title: Cabinet Office
text:
Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Cabinet Office 
Address: 70 Whitehall 
London 
SW1A 2AS 
  
 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant submitted a request to the Cabinet Office seeking all 
National Security Council records, minutes, readouts and handwritten 
notes relating to the potential proscription of Palestine Action. The 
Cabinet Office refused to confirm or deny whether it held the requested 
information on the basis of section 35(3) of FOIA by virtue of section 
35(1)(b) (Ministerial communications). 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that the Cabinet Office is entitled to rely 
on section 35(3) of FOIA to refuse to confirm or deny whether it holds 
the requested information. 
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps. 
Background 
4. The National Security Council (NSC) is the main forum for collective 
discussion of the government’s strategic approach to national security, 
foreign policy, resilience, international relations, economic security, 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 2 
trade, development, defence and global issues. It is chaired by the 
Prime Minister and is a Cabinet committee.1 
5. Palestine Action was proscribed under the Terrorism Act 2000 (TA 2000) 
on 5 July 2025. 
6. Palestine Action sought to challenge this decision2 and on 13 February 
2026 the High Court found the decision to proscribe Palestine Action was 
unlawful.3 
Request and response 
7. The complainant submitted the following request to the Cabinet Office 
on 10 July 2025: 
“Please provide  
 
1. All National Security Council (NSC) records, minutes, readouts, 
handwritten notes relating to the potential proscription of Palestine 
Action (2020-2025).” 
 
8. The Cabinet Office responded on 11 August 2025 and explained that:   
“We are writing to advise you that The Cabinet Office neither confirms 
nor denies that it holds information you have requested, under section 
35(1) of the Freedom of Information Act. The duty in section 1(1)(a) of 
the Act does not apply, by virtue of section 35(1)(a) of the Act.”   
 
9. The response went on to explain that the balance of the public interest 
test favoured maintaining the neither confirm nor deny (NCND) position. 
10. The complainant contacted the Cabinet Office on the same day and 
asked it to conduct an internal review of this refusal.  
11. The Cabinet Office informed him of the outcome of the internal review 
on 17 September 2025. This explained that it was satisfied that the 
exemption contained at section 35(3) of FOIA was engaged on the 
following basis: 
 
 
1 https://www.gov.uk/government/groups/national-security-council  
2 https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cqlerg1n67no  
3 https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c3wleezq73no  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 3 
“The information you have requested, if held (which is neither 
confirmed nor denied) would relate to Ministerial communications on 
the basis that you have sought information that concerns the NSC, 
which Ministers attend. It follows that the Cabinet Office neither 
confirms nor denies whether it holds information under section 35(3) of 
the Act.” 
 
12. The internal review response went on to explain that the Cabinet Office 
was satisfied that the public interest favoured maintaining the exclusion 
to confirm or deny whether information was held. 
Scope of the case 
13. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 17 September 2025 in 
order to complain about the Cabinet Office’s refusal of his request. 
14. During the course of the Commissioner’s investigation the Cabinet Office 
explained that it was also relying sections 23(5) (security bodies), 24(2) 
(national security) and 31(3) to refuse to confirm or deny whether it 
held any information falling within the scope of the request. 
15. It is important to recognise that the Commissioner’s role in considering 
the application of exemptions is limited to considering the circumstances 
at the time the request should have been responded to in accordance 
with the statutory timeframes for compliance set out in FOIA. That is, at 
the 20 working days limit and not the circumstances that exist at the 
point that the decision notice is being issued. 
16. Therefore whilst there have been developments in respect of the 
decision to proscribe Palestine Action as described in the Background 
section above, since these post-date 7 August 2025 (ie the twentieth 
working day after the request was submitted) these are not relevant to 
the Commissioner’s decision in respect of whether the exemptions have 
been applied correctly. 
Reasons for decision 
Section 35(3) 
17. Section 35(3) states that: 
“The duty to confirm or deny does not arise in relation to information 
which is (or if it were held by the public authority would be) exempt 
information by virtue of subsection (1).”  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 4 
18. The practical consequence of this is that if the requested information (if 
held) fell within the scope of any of the exemptions contained in section 
35(1) of FOIA then the Cabinet Office can refuse to confirm or deny 
whether it holds it, subject to a balance of public interest test. 
19. The Cabinet Office explained that the relevant exemption was section 
35(1)(b) which provides an exemption for information which relates to 
Ministerial communications. 
20. In support of this position the Cabinet Office explained that the 
information (if held) would constitute ministerial communications by 
virtue of section 35(5) of FOIA, which notes that ‘Ministerial 
communications’ includes the proceedings of the Cabinet or of any 
committee of the Cabinet, which would include the NSC. 
21. The Commissioner is satisfied that the requested information, if held, 
would relate to proceedings of the NSC, a Cabinet committee and 
therefore, if held, would fall within the exemption provided by section 
35(1)(b) as such information would relate to a form of ministerial 
communication. The Commissioner is therefore satisfied that section 
35(3) is engaged. 
Public interest test 
22. Section 35 is a qualified exemption and therefore the Commissioner 
must consider the public interest test and whether in all the 
circumstances of the case the public interest in maintaining the 
exemption outweighs the public interest in confirming whether or not 
the requested information is held. 
Public interest in maintaining the exemption 
23. The Cabinet Office provided the Commissioner with detailed 
submissions, set out under a number of different sub-headings, to 
support its position that the public interest favoured maintaining the 
exemption. The Commissioner has replicated these submissions below. 
The principle of Cabinet Collective Responsibility 
24. The Cabinet Office explained that the timing, attendance, agenda items 
and contents of Cabinet committee meetings are not normally put into 
the public domain with the Cabinet Office Manual noting at paragraph 
11.18 that:  
“the Government’s working assumption is that information relating to 
the proceedings of Cabinet and its committees should remain 
confidential.” 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 5 
25. The Cabinet Office argued that confirming whether or not it held the 
requested information would undermine the principle of Cabinet 
collective responsibility. 
26. It explained that the convention of Cabinet collective responsibility 
exists to underpin consistent and coherent government and has been a 
foundational pillar of the UK’s system of Cabinet government and the 
UK’s constitutional framework since the eighteenth century. The Cabinet 
Office argued that Parliament explicitly recognised the public interest in 
the maintenance of Cabinet collective responsibility, for example via 
section 36(2)(a)(i) and the provisions of section 35(3) of FOIA. 
27. The Cabinet Office explained that the principle is referred to in the 
Ministerial Code: 
“The principle of collective responsibility requires that ministers should 
be able to express their views frankly in the expectation that they can 
argue freely in private while maintaining a united front when decisions 
have been reached. This in turn requires that the privacy of opinions 
expressed in cabinet and ministerial committees, including in 
correspondence, should be maintained.” 
28. And: 
“The internal process through which a decision has been made, or the 
level of Committee by which it was taken should not be disclosed. 
Neither should the individual views of Ministers or advice provided by 
civil servants as part of that internal process be disclosed. Decisions 
reached by the Cabinet or Ministerial Committees are binding on all 
members of the Government.” 
29. The Cabinet Office explained that the same principles applied to the 
committees of the Cabinet with the Cabinet Manual noting that the 
Cabinet committee structure: 
“supports the principle of collective responsibility, ensuring that policy 
proposals receive thorough consideration without an issue having to be 
referred to the whole Cabinet.” 
30. The Cabinet Office argued that it was necessary to NCND whether it held 
the requested information because either response would amount to the 
disclosure of an agenda item of a Cabinet committee. The Cabinet Office 
argued that the logical conclusion of such a response would be to 
regularly disclose the business of Cabinet and its committees. In its view 
such disclosures would be prejudicial to the convention of collective 
responsibility in the following ways: 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 6 
• Disclosure would lead to a reduction in the candour of ministerial 
discussions in these fora as disclosure would contribute to public 
debate and speculation about the contributions of individual ministers 
to the meeting;  
• The routine disclosure of what subjects had been discussed by Cabinet 
committees would adversely affect the ability of ministers to organise 
themselves as they see fit to discharge the collective business of 
government in the most effective and efficient way, without regard to 
public commentary on the way in which collective agreement was 
sought, or speculation about the substantive content of ministerial 
discussions in these fora; and  
• The routine disclosure of Cabinet (and Cabinet committee) meeting 
agendas would undermine the operation of the convention of Cabinet 
collective responsibility. 
31. In the circumstances of this case the Cabinet Office argued that if it 
were to confirm or deny whether it held the requested information it 
would make it apparent whether or not the NSC had discussed the 
subject of Palestine Action. As noted above, in its view the logic of 
issuing such a response is that it would be possible, through a series of 
requests, to establish what the agenda of the NSC is on a regular basis, 
together with the agendas of the Cabinet and other Cabinet committees. 
The quality of discussions at Cabinet and its committees 
32. The Cabinet Office emphasised, again as noted above, that the agendas 
of Cabinet committees are not normally disclosed in order to protect the 
safe and private space for ministerial discussion in these fora. In its view 
confirmation that a Cabinet committee had (or had not) discussed a 
particular topic would undermine the quality of discussions in these 
settings. 
33. The Cabinet Office explained that aside from full Cabinet, Cabinet 
committees are the highest decision making bodies of the government. 
As a result, such meetings are subject to significant public interest, 
particularly on high profiles matters such as the proscription of Palestine 
Action. The Cabinet Office argued that disclosure of the agenda items 
would result in public speculation about the contributions of individuals 
at those meeting. In its view the public may make approximations about 
the contributions of individual ministers based on their public profiles as 
a politician, or their previous public pronouncements on these issues. As 
a result, the Cabinet Office argued that ministers may therefore feel 
incentivised to give contributions based on how they will be received by 
audiences outside of the room, beyond the requirements of frank, 
candid and effective discussion of all policy options. As a result, the 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 7 
quality of discussions at Cabinet committees would decline, leading to 
worse decision making outcomes. 
The Cabinet committee process 
34. The Cabinet Office also argued that the NCND response was necessary 
to protect the sovereignty of the collective deliberative process of 
Cabinet committees such as the NSC. The Cabinet Office explained that 
Cabinet committees help to ensure that government business is 
processed more effectively relieving pressure on Cabinet. The committee 
structure also supports the principle of Cabinet collective responsibility, 
ensuring that policy proposals receive thorough consideration without an 
issue having to be referred to the whole Cabinet. The Cabinet Office 
argued that ministers should be able to organise themselves as they see 
fit in order to order to discharge the collective business of government 
in the absence of premature public scrutiny. The Cabinet Office argued 
that revealing which subjects were disclosed at which meetings would 
result in ministers being incentivised to either increase or decrease the 
frequency of meetings, meet to discuss a particular issue to be seen to 
do so or choose not to meet to discuss certain controversial issues for 
risk of undue scrutiny. Consequently, agendas may become less candid 
and the efficiency and candour of discussions would decrease. 
35. The Cabinet Office added that ministers may seek to escalate matters to 
the Cabinet or a Cabinet committee in anticipation of being seen to raise 
them at either of these fora. Conversely, ministers may be mindful 
about bringing highly contentious subjects to a Cabinet committee 
because they would be mindful of the later public reaction when the fact 
of that subject being discussed becomes known. Furthermore, the 
Cabinet committee agendas are by their nature brief, which may in turn 
generate public speculation about what exactly the government’s plans 
are. 
36. The Cabinet Office also highlighted a scenario if a particular matter was 
acknowledged to have been discussed at multiple Cabinet committee 
meetings without it being apparent that a decision had been made, it 
would encourage unhelpful speculation that the Cabinet was too divided 
on the subject, that the Cabinet was incapable of making a decision over 
it or that the subject was more intractable than was actually the case. 
Operation of the convention of Cabinet Collective Responsibility in public 
37. A regular disclosure of items discussed at Cabinet (and Cabinet 
committees) could prematurely reveal what progress the government is 
(or is not, as they case may be) making in respect of the development 
of a certain policy. Inferences may be drawn if it were to be repeatedly 
confirmed that the Cabinet had not discussed a certain issue that had 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 8 
salience among the general public. It may also lead to conclusions being 
reached as to which subjects were being prioritised at Cabinet level, 
thereby encroaching on the ability of ministers to order their business as 
they see fit. 
38. As a result, a sufficient number of disclosures as to what subjects 
constituted the business of the Cabinet would effectively expose certain 
dynamics of the Cabinet. In such circumstances, it would be increasingly 
difficult to maintain Cabinet collective responsibility, which depends on 
there being a unified public position, which should not be undermined by 
disclosures which would encourage speculation as to the extent to which 
the Cabinet did (or did not) agree on certain subjects. 
The responsibility of departments 
39. The Cabinet Office argued that complying with section 1(1)(a) of FOIA 
would interfere with the responsibility of the government to inform the 
public as to how it is performing its functions and the decisions that it 
has made. 
40. Paragraph 4.43 of the Cabinet Manual notes that:  
“Ministers are responsible for ensuring that their departments take 
whatever action is necessary to implement decisions made by Cabinet 
or Cabinet committees, and for reporting back to colleagues on 
progress if needed.” 
41. The Cabinet Office explained that it was the responsibility of ministers 
and departments to carry out the decisions of Cabinet through 
legislation or otherwise and to advise the public the actions they taking 
following decisions made at Cabinet level. Confirmation or denial as to 
the subjects at Cabinet or Cabinet committees would effectively short 
cut that process. That is to say, the public would be aware of topics 
discussed at Cabinet before departments had established how decisions 
of Cabinet should be implemented or presented.   
42. The Cabinet Office argued that this would lead to considerable disruption 
or confusion. It highlighted that discussion of a topic may suggest to the 
public that the government had reached a decision on this, but that is 
not always the case. It is common practice for Cabinet committee 
meetings to decide to send matters back to departments for further 
work, or to escalate a matter to a different Cabinet committee meeting 
(attended by more senior ministers) or to the Cabinet itself. 
43. In certain circumstances, government departments also could feel under 
pressure to issue announcements before the fact of a certain subject 
having been discussed at Cabinet is disclosed. Confidence in a 
government department may be undermined if an announcement on a 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 9 
particular matter was expected while it had, in fact, been sent back to 
the Cabinet (or Cabinet committee) for a further decision. In such 
circumstances, the management of public expectations in respect of 
particular policy areas could become very difficult. 
44. The Cabinet Office highlighted that minutes are part of the historic 
record of government and are handled in line with the Public Records 
Act 1958. If the government considered it beneficial to do so it would 
regularly publish the agenda items of Cabinet meetings. However, it did 
not do so and instead there is a 20 year period before such minutes to 
The National Archives so they can form part of the public record. 
Public interest in confirming or denying whether the information is held 
45. The Cabinet Office acknowledged that there is a public interest in there 
being greater transparency around the matter of how government is 
conducted at the highest level and in understanding what the Cabinet 
(and Cabinet committees) discusses. 
46. The complainant argued that the public interest outweighed the public 
interest in maintaining the exemption because the requested 
information related to a decision by Cabinet ministers to proscribe what 
was previously defined as a protest group. 
47. The complainant argued that this decision caused unprecedented impact 
on ECHR article 10 and 11 human rights and fundamental freedoms of 
the entire population of the UK. He argued that it was of vital public 
interest that the public have access to information on how this decision 
was made, who supported it, and on what grounds it was made. In his 
view this required confirmation or denial of NSC involvement, and if 
confirmed, full disclosure of all related records. 
48. The complainant argued that it is already in the public domain that the 
Home Secretary made the decision to proscribe Palestine Action after 
consulting other government departments such as the Foreign, 
Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) and Department for 
Business and Trade (DBT), as well as the security services. He 
suggested that it would therefore not be particularly revealing to 
discover the issue was discussed by the NSC (if indeed that were the 
case). 
49. The complainant also noted that that there was a long history of the 
government of the day raising protest issues in Cabinet committee 
meetings, such as the MISC 13 Cabinet Committee on Animal Rights 
Activists 2001-2006 (see footnote 5 below). 
  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 10 
Balance of the public interest arguments 
50. The Commissioner accepts that it is for the Cabinet and the government 
to determine the level at which matters are discussed and that process, 
in itself, deserves protection. Furthermore, the Commissioner also 
accepts that if confirmation or denial of the level at which decisions are 
made were routinely provided, this would inevitably lead to increased 
pressure on the government over process issues in terms of the 
organisation of Cabinet and Cabinet committee business and that this 
would be contrary to the public interest for the reasons identified by the 
Cabinet Office. In addition, the Commissioner accepts that there is a 
significant public interest in maintaining and respecting the principle of 
collective Cabinet responsibility. Confirmation as to whether or not the 
requested information is held would undermine that principle by 
revealing whether ministers had discussed a particular issue and within 
a particular forum. As a result the Commissioner accepts that there is 
significant public interest in maintaining the exemption. The 
Commissioner notes that accepting that such weight applies to these 
arguments in this case is in line with his findings in other cases in 
involving requests which sought to establish whether certain topics had 
been discussed at Cabinet or Cabinet Committees.4 
51. With regard to the public interest in favour of confirming or denying 
whether the Cabinet Office holds any information, the Commissioner 
acknowledges that doing so would add, to some degree, to the public’s 
understanding of the decision making process regarding the proscribing 
of Palestine Action. Furthermore, the Commissioner recognises this 
decision has attracted considerable public attention, and along with 
criticism, and at the point that the request was submitted was the 
subject of legal challenge. He accepts that such factors add weight to 
the public interest in confirming whether or not this matter was 
discussed at the NSC in order to potentially provide some transparency 
and clarity as to the forum and level within government at which this 
decision may have been reached.  
52. However, in the Commissioner’s view there is a limit to how informative 
such a confirmation or denial would be. As the Cabinet Office has set 
out, regardless as to the level at which decisions may have been taken – 
once a decision has been made this then becomes the government’s 
position which ministers, in line with the convention of Cabinet collective 
responsibility, then support. Moreover, as the Cabinet Office noted it is 
the role of the relevant department – in this case the Home Office – to 
 
 
4 FS50601833, FS50535179, IC-76534-Q2X7 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 11 
translate any such decision which is taken into action. Therefore, whilst 
compliance with section 1(1)(a) would arguably make the government’s 
decision making process more transparent, beyond the high profile 
nature of the proscription, the Commissioner is not clear why confirming 
whether or not this decision was discussed specifically at NSC is so 
pressing. This is because ultimately this decision was the government’s 
decision, not simply the decision of one of its Cabinet committees which 
may (or may) have discussed the matter. 
53. The Commissioner notes that the complainant argued that it is in the 
public domain that the Home Secretary decided to proscribe Palestine 
Action after consulting other government departments such as the FCDO 
and DBT and also after consulting the security services. The 
Commissioner has not been provided with any sources which pre-date 
the request (or are dated up to 7 August 2025) to support this point. 
However, even if this were the case the Commissioner considers that 
any confirmation that the Home Secretary discussed the matter with 
such bodies to be materially different from a confirmation that the 
matter may have been discussed at the NSC, not least because any such 
engagement with such bodies could have taken place outside of the 
NSC.  
54. With regard to the complainant’s reference to the MISC13 Cabinet 
Committee, the Commissioner notes that this was a entirely different 
committee, the Ministerial Committee on Animal Rights Activists (and 
subsequently Ministerial Committee on Animal Rights Extremism).5 He 
does not consider that simply because the remit of such a committee – 
who’s role as the name suggests - was to consider protest groups, that 
this equates to an expectation that 20 years later the NSC would also be 
a forum within which specific protest groups are likely to be discussed.  
55. Taking the above into account, whilst the Commissioner accepts that 
confirming whether or not the NSC discussed this issue would contribute 
to transparency in respect of this, and whilst he acknowledges the 
controversial nature of the decision, in the Commissioner’s view such 
interests are significantly outweighed by the public interest in 
maintaining the exemption given the impact this would have on the 
government’s decision making processes more broadly. Consequently, 
the Commissioner has found that the public interest favours maintaining 
the exemption contained at section 35(3). 
 
 
5 https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2006-06-
29/debates/06062967000024/MinisterialCommitteesOfTheCabinet(MISC13)  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 12 
56. In view of this decision the Commissioner has not considered the 
Cabinet Office’s application of the other exemptions it cited to refuse this 
request. 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 13 
Right of appeal  
57. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights)   
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
58. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
59. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
 
Jonathan Slee 
Senior Case Officer 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF
description: The complainant submitted a request to the Cabinet Office seeking all National Security Council records, minutes, readouts and handwritten notes relating to the potential proscription of Palestine Action. The Cabinet Office refused to confirm or deny whether it held the requested information on the basis of section 35(3) of FOIA by virtue of section 35(1)(b) (Ministerial communications). The Commissioner’s decision is that the Cabinet Office is entitled to rely on section 35(3) of FOIA to refuse to confirm or deny whether it holds the requested information.
